10 Testing React — Test Behavior, Not Implementation

← Back to lessons

Most test suites are brittle because they test the wrong things. They test that a component has a certain CSS class, not that a user can complete a task. This lesson teaches the Testing Library philosophy and practical patterns for testing real applications.

The Testing Philosophy: User-Centric Tests

Kent C. Dodds' principle: "The more your tests resemble the way your software is used, the more confidence they can give you."

This means:

  • Don't test that a component rendered a div with className article-card
  • Do test that an article's title and author are visible on the page
  • Don't test that useState was called with the right initial value
  • Do test that clicking "Add to cart" shows the item in the cart

The journalist test: If you refactor a component's internals (change div to section, rename a state variable, restructure the JSX) and the user experience doesn't change, your tests should still pass. If they break, they're testing implementation details — like fact-checking your notes instead of your published story. The reader doesn't care about your notes. They care about the story.

Testing Library: Queries and Interactions

Priority of queries (most to least accessible)

PriorityQueryWhen to Use
1stgetByRoleButtons, links, headings, inputs (accessible to everyone)
2ndgetByLabelTextForm fields (how users find inputs)
3rdgetByPlaceholderTextWhen no label exists
4thgetByTextNon-interactive elements with visible text
5thgetByDisplayValueFilled-in form fields
LastgetByTestIdEscape hatch when nothing else works
// Testing a search component
import { render, screen } from '@testing-library/react';
import userEvent from '@testing-library/user-event';
import { ArticleSearch } from './ArticleSearch';

describe('ArticleSearch', () => {
  it('filters articles as the user types', async () => {
    const user = userEvent.setup();
    render(<ArticleSearch articles={mockArticles} />);

    // Query by role — how a screen reader sees it
    const searchInput = screen.getByRole('searchbox', { name: /search/i });

    // Simulate real user typing (not fireEvent.change!)
    await user.type(searchInput, 'climate');

    // Assert on what the user sees
    expect(screen.getByText('Climate Report: 2026 Update')).toBeInTheDocument();
    expect(screen.queryByText('Budget Bill Passes Senate')).not.toBeInTheDocument();
  });

  it('shows empty state when no results match', async () => {
    const user = userEvent.setup();
    render(<ArticleSearch articles={mockArticles} />);

    const searchInput = screen.getByRole('searchbox', { name: /search/i });
    await user.type(searchInput, 'xyznonexistent');

    expect(screen.getByText(/no articles found/i)).toBeInTheDocument();
  });
});

userEvent vs fireEvent: Always prefer userEvent. It simulates real user interactions: user.type() fires focus, keyDown, keyPress, input, keyUp events for each character — just like a real keyboard. fireEvent.change() only fires the change event, skipping everything else. Tests with userEvent catch more bugs because they exercise the full event lifecycle.

Component Testing Patterns

Testing with providers

// test/utils.tsx — custom render with providers
import { render, RenderOptions } from '@testing-library/react';
import { QueryClient, QueryClientProvider } from '@tanstack/react-query';
import { ThemeProvider } from '@/contexts/theme';

function createTestQueryClient() {
  return new QueryClient({
    defaultOptions: {
      queries: { retry: false },  // Don't retry in tests
    },
  });
}

function AllProviders({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const queryClient = createTestQueryClient();
  return (
    <QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}>
      <ThemeProvider>
        {children}
      </ThemeProvider>
    </QueryClientProvider>
  );
}

const customRender = (ui: React.ReactElement, options?: RenderOptions) =>
  render(ui, { wrapper: AllProviders, ...options });

export { customRender as render, screen } from '@testing-library/react';

Testing async components

// Testing a component that fetches data
import { render, screen, waitFor } from '@/test/utils';

describe('ArticleList', () => {
  it('shows loading state, then articles', async () => {
    render(<ArticleList />);

    // Initially shows loading
    expect(screen.getByText(/loading/i)).toBeInTheDocument();

    // Wait for data to appear
    await waitFor(() => {
      expect(screen.getByText('Climate Report')).toBeInTheDocument();
    });

    // Loading state is gone
    expect(screen.queryByText(/loading/i)).not.toBeInTheDocument();
  });

  it('shows error state when fetch fails', async () => {
    // MSW will return an error for this test (see next section)
    server.use(
      http.get('/api/articles', () => HttpResponse.error())
    );

    render(<ArticleList />);

    await waitFor(() => {
      expect(screen.getByText(/failed to load/i)).toBeInTheDocument();
    });

    // Test the retry button
    const retryButton = screen.getByRole('button', { name: /try again/i });
    expect(retryButton).toBeInTheDocument();
  });
});

MSW: Mock Service Worker for API Testing

MSW intercepts network requests at the service worker level. Your components make real fetch calls — MSW intercepts them and returns mock data. The component doesn't know it's being mocked.

// mocks/handlers.ts
import { http, HttpResponse } from 'msw';

export const handlers = [
  // GET /api/articles
  http.get('/api/articles', ({ request }) => {
    const url = new URL(request.url);
    const category = url.searchParams.get('category');

    const articles = mockArticles.filter(
      a => !category || a.category === category
    );

    return HttpResponse.json({ articles });
  }),

  // POST /api/articles/:id/like
  http.post('/api/articles/:id/like', ({ params }) => {
    return HttpResponse.json({ liked: true, id: params.id });
  }),

  // Simulate slow response
  http.get('/api/slow-data', async () => {
    await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 2000));
    return HttpResponse.json({ data: 'slow' });
  }),
];

// mocks/server.ts — for tests
import { setupServer } from 'msw/node';
import { handlers } from './handlers';

export const server = setupServer(...handlers);

// vitest.setup.ts
beforeAll(() => server.listen());
afterEach(() => server.resetHandlers());
afterAll(() => server.close());

Why MSW beats manual mocking: When you mock fetch directly or mock a data hook, you're testing your mock, not your code. The actual fetch logic, error handling, and request formatting are skipped. MSW lets your real code run end-to-end. If your component builds a URL wrong or doesn't handle a 404 response, MSW-based tests catch it. Manual mocks don't.

Testing Forms

// Testing a form with validation
describe('ArticleForm', () => {
  it('shows validation errors for empty required fields', async () => {
    const user = userEvent.setup();
    render(<ArticleForm />);

    // Submit without filling anything
    const submitButton = screen.getByRole('button', { name: /publish/i });
    await user.click(submitButton);

    // Check for validation errors
    expect(screen.getByText(/title is required/i)).toBeInTheDocument();
    expect(screen.getByText(/article body is required/i)).toBeInTheDocument();
  });

  it('submits successfully with valid data', async () => {
    const user = userEvent.setup();
    render(<ArticleForm />);

    // Fill in the form
    await user.type(
      screen.getByLabelText(/title/i),
      'New Climate Report Released'
    );
    await user.type(
      screen.getByLabelText(/body/i),
      'A comprehensive new climate report was released today showing...'
    );
    await user.selectOptions(
      screen.getByLabelText(/category/i),
      'politics'
    );

    // Submit
    await user.click(screen.getByRole('button', { name: /publish/i }));

    // Verify success (MSW handles the API call)
    await waitFor(() => {
      expect(screen.getByText(/published successfully/i)).toBeInTheDocument();
    });
  });
});

Playwright: End-to-End Testing

Playwright tests run in a real browser against your running application. They test the full stack: frontend, API, database (or MSW).

// e2e/article-workflow.spec.ts
import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';

test.describe('Article publishing workflow', () => {
  test('user can create, edit, and delete an article', async ({ page }) => {
    // Navigate to article creation
    await page.goto('/articles/new');

    // Fill in the form
    await page.getByLabel('Title').fill('Test Article for E2E');
    await page.getByLabel('Body').fill('This is a test article body...');
    await page.getByLabel('Category').selectOption('tech');

    // Submit
    await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Publish' }).click();

    // Verify redirect to article page
    await expect(page).toHaveURL(/\/articles\/test-article-for-e2e/);
    await expect(page.getByRole('heading', { level: 1 }))
      .toHaveText('Test Article for E2E');

    // Edit the article
    await page.getByRole('link', { name: 'Edit' }).click();
    await page.getByLabel('Title').fill('Updated E2E Article');
    await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Save' }).click();

    // Verify update
    await expect(page.getByRole('heading', { level: 1 }))
      .toHaveText('Updated E2E Article');

    // Delete
    await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Delete' }).click();
    await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Confirm' }).click();

    // Verify redirect to articles list
    await expect(page).toHaveURL('/articles');
  });
});

The Testing Decision Framework

Test TypeWhat to TestToolSpeed
UnitUtility functions, hooks, schemasVitest~ms
ComponentUI behavior, user interactionsTesting Library + Vitest~100ms
IntegrationComponent + API interactionTesting Library + MSW~200ms
E2ECritical user workflowsPlaywright~seconds

What NOT to test

  • Third-party library behavior (Tailwind classes, React Query internals)
  • Implementation details (state variable names, internal component structure)
  • Snapshot tests of large components (brittle, low signal, high maintenance)
  • Every possible input combination (test boundaries and edge cases, not every variation)

The testing pyramid, pragmatically: Write many unit tests for business logic (fast, stable). Write component tests for key user interactions (medium). Write a few E2E tests for critical paths (sign up, purchase, publish). If you only have time for one kind, write integration tests with MSW — they give the most confidence per test.

Check Your Understanding

1. Why should you prefer getByRole over getByTestId?

getByRole queries the way assistive technology (screen readers) sees your UI. If you can find an element by role, it means your UI is accessible. getByTestId requires adding test-specific attributes that real users and assistive tech don't use. Role queries simultaneously test functionality and accessibility.

2. Why use MSW instead of mocking fetch directly?

MSW intercepts at the network level, so your real fetch/axios code runs completely. It tests your URL construction, headers, error handling, and response parsing. Mocking fetch skips all of that code, testing only what happens after you get data. MSW also shares handlers between dev, tests, and Storybook.

3. What's wrong with snapshot tests for component output?

They're brittle and low-signal. Any change to the HTML structure (adding a div, changing a class name, reordering attributes) fails the snapshot, even if the user experience is identical. Developers update snapshots reflexively without reviewing them, which defeats the purpose. Test specific behavior instead.

4. You refactored a component from using useState to useReducer. Should your tests break?

No! If the user experience didn't change, tests shouldn't break. That's the whole point of testing behavior over implementation. If your tests broke because they were checking internal state names or hook calls, they were testing implementation details.

Exercise: Build a Test Suite

  1. Write MSW handlers for a mock articles API (CRUD operations)
  2. Write component tests for ArticleSearch: test typing, filtering, empty state, error state
  3. Write form tests for ArticleForm: validation errors, successful submission, server errors
  4. Write one E2E test with Playwright: the full create-edit-delete workflow
  5. Run coverage. Identify what's untested. Is it worth testing?
← Lesson 09: Forms Lesson 11: Framework Comparison →
